items.title: Mythos surfaces in Claude Code as Anthropic's Glasswing update logs 23,019 vulnerabilities — wolfSSL ships 5.9.1 patch, maintainers ask Anthropic to slow down
items.summary: Twenty-four hours of follow-up coverage has tightened the picture around Anthropic's May 22 Project Glasswing partner-scan update. Claude Mythos Preview has now identified 23,019 potential vulnerabilities across more than 1,000 open-source projects in roughly a month, with 6,202 classified as high- or critical-severity; six independent firms have validated more than 90% of 1,752 assessed findings as true positives. The most-cited single finding, a critical certificate-forgery flaw in the wolfSSL embedded TLS library (CVE-2026-5194, CVSS 9.1), has now been patched in wolfSSL 5.9.1 — wolfSSL's own write-up confirms Anthropic pointed Mythos at the codebase and that the run generated eight CVEs, and Anthropic plans to publish a full technical analysis of the forge-cert exploit. Cloudflare separately reported 2,000 bugs (400 high- or critical-severity) at a false-positive rate the company says is better than human testers; Mozilla used Mythos to identify and fix 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox 150. The May 22 Anthropic post (https://www.anthropic.com/research/glasswing-initial-update) also formally lifted the Glasswing NDA, letting partners publicly disclose their participation and share Mythos-built tools, findings, and best practices (Insurance Journal, Security Boulevard May 26). The most consequential new framing in the May 26-27 follow-ups (TechBooky, gHacks, The Next Web, TechZine, GBHackers): Anthropic publicly stated it plans a future public release of Mythos-class models once safeguards are ready, while several OSS maintainers have now asked Anthropic to slow the pace of disclosures — a high- or critical-severity Mythos finding takes an average of two weeks to patch, and Anthropic has disclosed 530 high- or critical-severity bugs to maintainers so far, of which 75 are patched and 65 have public advisories (a separate per-finding count tracks 1,596 reports, 1,451 acknowledged, 97 patched, 88 advisories). Separately, the 'claude-mythos-1-preview' toggle sighting in the public Claude Code interface on May 25-26 (WinBuzzer, Testing Catalog, Cyber Security News) remains the cleanest near-term productization signal: Anthropic is preparing a commercial 'Mythos 1' for Claude Code and a revamped Claude Security enterprise dashboard, and Nikkei Asia / TNW still report Japan's three megabanks (MUFG, SMBC, Mizuho) as the first Asian financial institutions in line for the restricted preview within roughly two weeks. The cross-coverage framing has consolidated overnight from 'can Mythos find them?' to 'patching is the bottleneck and maintainers are already telling Anthropic to slow down.'
items.why_it_matters: Two things in this story are worth absorbing as it develops. First is the rollout signal: the Claude Code UI sighting plus Japan-megabanks preview plus Anthropic now publicly stating it plans to release Mythos-class models once safeguards are ready is the full 'restricted research preview → select enterprise customers → Claude Code enterprise tier → broader API' pattern showing up at once. If 'claude-mythos-1' becomes a stable string in the Claude Code changelog, broader access is likely a month or two out. Second is what Mythos actually does, now that the partner-program numbers are public and the first patch has shipped: it autonomously finds and builds working exploits for real vulnerabilities in real shipped software, and the wolfSSL CVE-2026-5194 patch (wolfSSL 5.9.1) is now the first end-to-end public proof point — Mythos found it, Anthropic disclosed it, the maintainer shipped a fixed version. The maintainer-pushback story is the more interesting one for a coder to think about: capability outran the disclosure-and-patch pipeline so fast that OSS maintainers are now telling a frontier lab to slow down, and the average 2-week patch time on a high-severity finding from Mythos is now the rate-limiting step on the whole disclosure pipeline. For your stack, the practical implication: if you ever use Claude for code review or security scanning on a Python or web project, this is the capability that will eventually arrive in your API — but the more durable problem-solving lesson is that the bottleneck moves once a tool gets fast enough, and triaging and patching 6,000 high-severity findings is now a more interesting engineering problem than finding them.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Mythos's wolfSSL CVE has now been patched in wolfSSL 5.9.1 and OSS maintainers are asking Anthropic to slow down — the disclosure pipeline, not the model, is the rate-limiting step.

revisions.change: promoted from am with afternoon context: May 26 day-of coverage (Help Net Security, SecurityWeek, The Hacker News, Engadget, Security Boulevard, TNW) of the May 22 Glasswing partner-scan update — 23,019 total issues across 1,000+ OSS projects, 6,202 high/critical, 90%+ true-positive rate across 1,752 independent assessments, wolfSSL CVE-2026-5194 (CVSS 9.1) forge-cert exploit, Cloudflare 2K bugs, Mozilla Firefox 150 +271 fixes, only 97 patched (humans-are-bottleneck framing); also folded in the parallel May 22 policy change lifting the Glasswing NDA. Updated title, summary, why_it_matters, brief, and added five new takes.

added overnight follow-up substance: wolfSSL has now shipped wolfSSL 5.9.1 patching the Mythos-found CVE (and reports the run produced 8 CVEs total in wolfSSL); Anthropic publicly stated it plans a future public release of Mythos-class models once safeguards are ready (TechBooky, gHacks May 26-27); several OSS maintainers have now asked Anthropic to slow the disclosure rate, with an average 2-week patch time on high-severity findings; refined Anthropic-reported numbers (530 high/critical disclosures, 75 patched, 65 advisories alongside the broader 1,596 reports / 1,451 acknowledged / 97 patched / 88 advisories tracker). Updated title (added wolfSSL 5.9.1 + maintainer-pushback hook), summary, why_it_matters, and brief; switched source to Anthropic's own May 22 research post; added three new takes (TechBooky, gHacks, The Next Web).

takes.take: Reports 'claude-mythos-1-preview' appeared in both the public Claude Code and Claude Security interfaces before being pulled, and describes Anthropic's phased approach: gated enterprise dashboard integration first, then measured expansion as safety evaluations mature.

Reports MUFG, SMBC, and Mizuho are set to receive Mythos access within approximately two weeks — the first Asian financial institutions in the restricted preview — following Treasury Secretary Bessent's Tokyo meetings.

Lays out the day-of numbers: 23,019 issues found across 1,000+ OSS projects, 6,202 high- or critical-severity, and the 90%+ true-positive rate that six independent firms validated across 1,752 assessed findings.
Centers on the wolfSSL CVE-2026-5194 (CVSS 9.1) certificate-forgery exploit Mythos constructed end-to-end, framing it as the proof-of-capability finding — wolfSSL is in billions of devices.

Picks up the 'capability has outrun the patch pipeline' framing — only 97 of the 10,000+ high-severity findings have been patched, and the bottleneck has moved from discovery to verification, disclosure, and fix.

Highlights the lifted-NDA policy change that lets Glasswing partners publicly disclose their participation and share Mythos-built tools, findings, and best practices with industry, regulators, OSS maintainers, and the press.

Centers the maintainer-pushback angle: capacity-constrained OSS maintainers have asked Anthropic to slow the pace of disclosures, with a two-week average patch time on high-severity findings now the rate-limiting step on the whole pipeline.

Confirms Anthropic publicly stated it plans a future public release of Mythos-class models once safeguards are ready — adds the policy-level rollout signal to the Claude Code UI-sighting and Japan-megabanks productization signals.

Lays out Anthropic's own framing: Mythos-class capabilities will eventually be released publicly, but only after the safeguards required to keep them from being used against the same software they're finding bugs in are mature enough.

items.title: DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus pairs Gemini 3.1 Pro with the Lean checker — Hassabis calls earlier OpenAI Erdős claim 'embarrassing,' says this is still not AGI
items.summary: In an arXiv preprint posted May 21 (2605.22763), Google DeepMind described AlphaProof Nexus, a formal-proof search system that pairs Gemini 3.1 Pro with the Lean proof assistant in an 'agentic loop' — the LLM proposes a proof, Lean mechanically checks every logical step, and the system iterates until Lean either accepts the proof or the loop gives up. Run on 353 open Erdős problems, it solved 9; on 492 open conjectures from the Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences, it proved 44; it also resolved a 15-year-old open question on Hilbert functions and tightened a bound in convex optimization. The cost per solved problem is on the order of a few hundred dollars. The Lean proofs and accompanying natural-language summaries are published in the public github.com/google-deepmind/alphaproof-nexus-results repository. The most substantive overnight context comes from DeepMind itself: in coverage picked up by WION, MSN, Techlusive, and Mlq.ai, CEO Demis Hassabis publicly framed AlphaProof Nexus as 'still not AGI' and used the launch to call out the earlier (October 2025) OpenAI claim — made by then-VP Kevin Weil — that GPT-5 had solved 10 previously-unsolved Erdős problems as 'embarrassing,' since researchers later showed the 'solved' problems already had published solutions and the model had only surfaced existing literature. The paper backs up the architectural point with a direct head-to-head: three runs of an 'OpenAI-style' chain-of-thought reasoning agent on the same formalized Erdős set produced zero accepted proofs, while AlphaProof Nexus's verifier-gated loop produced 9. WinBuzzer's May 26 piece — 'AI math race moves beyond benchmarks' — captures the broader shift, and Mlq.ai notes the system is already being applied to ongoing research in quantum optics and graph theory. The framing is the cleanest one yet on why LLM-plus-verifier is the architecture that's actually shipping original research: Lean decides what leaves the loop, the LLM is allowed to be wrong, and the cost-per-proof figure (hundreds of dollars) makes it economic to throw the system at long tails of small open problems and collect what falls out. This is the obvious DeepMind counterpart to OpenAI's separate May 20 announcement that an internal reasoning model autonomously disproved the planar unit-distance Erdős conjecture (covered here May 22) — that one is a single big result without a verifier; AlphaProof Nexus is many smaller results with a verifier.
items.why_it_matters: The fastest-growing-but-most-overlooked architectural pattern in 2026 is LLM-plus-verifier: the model is allowed to hallucinate freely, but a deterministic checker decides what leaves the loop. AlphaProof Nexus is the clean academic version of the same pattern you already rely on without naming it when Claude writes Python and you run it — Lean is to math what running the code is to programming. The head-to-head in the paper sharpens the point: three runs of an 'OpenAI-style' chain-of-thought agent on the same formalized Erdős problems produced zero proofs, while the Lean-gated loop produced nine. That gap — same model class, different scaffolding — is the load-bearing detail to absorb, because the same shape shows up wherever agents are getting more reliable: a compiler, a unit-test runner, a type checker, a linter, even a JSON schema validator. The Hassabis 'still not AGI' framing and the 'embarrassing' callback to OpenAI's October 2025 Weil-deleted Erdős claim are useful for situational awareness — DeepMind is positioning the verifier-in-the-loop architecture not just as a different research direction but as the credible-original-result one, contrasted against natural-language chain-of-thought claims that have historically collapsed under scrutiny. The cost-per-proof figure (hundreds of dollars) is the second load-bearing detail: at that price point, throwing the system at a long tail of small open problems and collecting what falls out is now economic, and the same logic will apply to the long tail of small engineering problems as soon as the verifier-in-the-loop architecture ships in a developer tool. For HTML/CSS/Python work the analogy is exact, and once you see the pattern you'll start noticing it everywhere agents are getting reliable.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Three runs of an OpenAI-style chain-of-thought agent solved zero formalized Erdős problems; the Lean-gated loop solved nine — same model class, the verifier is what makes the architecture work.

revisions.change: added substantive overnight context: Hassabis publicly framed the system as 'still not AGI' and called the October 2025 OpenAI Kevin-Weil-deleted '10 Erdős problems solved' claim 'embarrassing'; surfaced the paper's direct head-to-head experiment in which three runs of an OpenAI-style chain-of-thought agent on the same formalized Erdős problems produced zero accepted proofs vs nine from the Lean-gated loop; noted ongoing application of AlphaProof Nexus to quantum optics and graph theory research (Mlq.ai). Updated title (added Hassabis framing hook), summary, why_it_matters, and brief; added three new takes (WION, Techlusive, Mlq.ai).

takes.take: Centers DeepMind's own 'still not AGI' framing and reads the paper as evidence that the AI math race has moved past benchmark performance into producing original results that hold up to formal verification.

Leads on the cost-per-proof figure — a few hundred dollars per resolved conjecture — and treats it as the threshold that makes large-scale automated proof search economically practical for the first time.
Lays out the headline result set: 9 of 353 open Erdős problems solved, 44 of 492 open OEIS sequence conjectures proved, plus a 15-year Hilbert-functions question resolved.
Frames it explicitly as an agent — 'AlphaProof Nexus agent' — and stresses the iterate-against-Lean architecture as the reason the results are checkable rather than just claimed.

Publishes the actual Lean proofs and natural-language prose proofs in a public repository — anyone with Lean can re-verify the results, which is what separates this release from earlier AI-math claims.

Foregrounds Hassabis's 'still not AGI' framing and his pointed reference to the earlier OpenAI Erdős claim that collapsed when researchers found the 'solved' problems already had published solutions — positioning AlphaProof Nexus as the credible verifier-gated alternative.
Picks up the same Hassabis line and reads it as DeepMind deliberately tempering expectations to contrast with rival labs' framing — original mathematics is a milestone, not AGI.

Surfaces the paper's direct head-to-head experiment — three runs of an OpenAI-style chain-of-thought agent on the same formalized Erdős problems produced zero accepted proofs — and notes the system is already being applied to ongoing research in quantum optics and graph theory.
